question: Define a C++ class named `Book` with private member variables for `title` (string), `author` (string), and `isbn` (string). Implement public methods to `setTitle`, `setAuthor`, `setISBN` and `displayBookInfo` which prints all details.

test_cases.expected:
Title: The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy
Author: Douglas Adams
ISBN: 978-0345391803

errors: The `setTitle`, `setAuthor`, and `setISBN` methods have parameters with the same names as the class's member variables (e.g., `void setTitle(const std::string& title)`). This causes parameter shadowing, meaning the assignments like `title = title;` within the methods modify the local parameter variable itself instead of the class's member variable. Consequently, the member variables remain uninitialized or default-constructed, violating the semantic intent of the setters.
